en.extract:
The Chaco eagle (Buteogallus coronatus) or crowned solitary eagle, is an endangered bird of prey from eastern and central South America. Typically it is known simply as the crowned eagle, which leads to potential confusion with the African Stephanoaetus coronatus.  Due to its rarity, not much is known about its biology or population.


Description
The Chaco eagle is a large raptor with a mostly dull and silvery grey body plumage. Its short black tail has a broad white medial band as well as a white tip and both its cere and legs are yellow. As its name indicates, the Crowned Solitary Eagle has a crest of darker grey feathers. The juvenile also has a crest but its colour dark brown, except for it creamy head and underbody which are both have grey streaks. It is one of the largest raptors in the Neotropics and normally weighs around 2.95 kg (6.5 lb). Its total length can reach 73–79 cm (29–31 in) and it has a wingspan of 170–183 cm (5 ft 7 in – 6 ft 0 in).


Taxonomy

Buteogallus coronatus is monotypic, with no subspecies. Vieillot first named it Harpyia coronata in 1817. It was subsequently reclassified by Lafresnaye in 1842 in the genus Harpyphaliatus, as Harpyhaliatus coronatus. At the time, the Solitary eagle (now Buteogallus solitarius) was considered to be a subspecies of Harpyhaliatus coronatus, until phylogenetic analyses of their DNA showed that they are, in fact, sister species. These analyses also indicated that another species, the Great black hawk (Buteogallus anthracinus), is also a sister species. They were all reclassified in the genus Buteogallus.


Habitat and distribution


Distribution
This bird is endemic to eastern and central South America, where it is present in southern and central Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay and in Argentina south to northern Patagonia. The status of the Chaco Eagle is uncertain in Uruguay, where it has not been seen for decades and is believed to be extinct. It has become regionally extinct in portions of its historical range, and is found in very low density throughout its range, making it one of the rarest and most threatened raptors in the Neotropical region. Only 250-999 mature individuals are left, while the total individual count of the species reaches 375-1499 birds.


Habitat
The Chaco Eagle can be found in semi open habitats such as open mixed grasslands, brushlands, savannahs, marshes and open woodlands. Also, the presence of large trees in the habitat is very important for the Chaco eagle since they are used for nesting.


Threats
The Chaco Eagle is considered endangered. Its population is declining, a trend that is most likely due to human activities. The Chaco Eagle populations are affected by many threats, including habitat degradation, collisions with human structures, and persecution. An example of the latter would be in Argentina, where Chaco Eagles are still killed due to the belief that they attack livestock. Between 1999 and 2014, 30 Chaco Eagles were brought to a rehabilitation center after they had been shot to protect livestock. For such a small population, this can have a great impact on the species' chances at survival. This persecution attracted the attention of conservation groups, which lead to the creation of a national plan of plan of conservation for the Chaco Eagle in Argentina, the only country to do so so far. Another important factor is the electrocution of Chaco Eagles by power poles, which also has a sizeable impact on their populations. This is also a common problem that unfortunately affects many raptors.


Behavior
Often seen in solitary pairs or small groups of three, the Chaco Eagle is a tame crepuscular species that forages during early hours.


Vocalization
The Chaco Eagle can be recognized by its very powerful and shrill whistling call that can be heard 2 km away.


Diet
Chaco Eagles still-hunt from perches. Its diet consists mostly of small to medium size mammals. It primarily preys on armadillos such as pichis (Zaedyus pichiy) and nine-banded armadillos (Dasypus novemcinctus) as well as Andes skunks (Conepatus chinga). But other mammals such as rodents, possums, European hares (Lepus europaeus), and lesser grisons (Galictis cuja) are also taken. Reptiles such as snakes, tortoises, and lizards also make up a smaller part of their diet. When hunting snakes, the eagles often remove the head first. Singular scales on the eagle's tarsus protected them from snake bites. Small lizards are caught directly with talons, but large lizards such as red tegus (Salvator rufescens) and Argentine black and white tegus (Tupinambis merianae) are captured by approaching them from the side, grabbing them by the hip with one foot and then by the head with the other, apparently to reduce the risk of being bitten or whipped by the tail. Normally, this raptor kills or hunts its prey, but some individuals have been seen eating dead deer, sheep, armadillos, and roadkill.


Reproduction

The Chaco Eagle is a monogamous species. Between August and October, a single white egg with grey spots is laid on a platform-like nest of sticks and leaves. The nest is constructed on the tallest trees of breeding territories, 10-13m above ground. Some eagles have been seen nesting on human made buildings in area where no trees were available. The egg hatches around 45 days after it was laid (November–December). The female stays near its offspring from the moment the egg is laid until the chick is old enough to be left alone for short periods of time. The male continuously brings food to the nest, while the female joins the hunting when the chick hatches and grows a bit. The offspring will stay in the nest for 65–70 days. Not much is known concerning the age of breeding, but it is estimated that they do not breed before they reach 3–5 years of age.


References


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet Archived 2009-01-02 at the Wayback Machine

The '''Chaco eagle''' ('''''Buteogallus coronatus''''') or '''crowned solitary eagle''', is an endangered [[bird of prey]] from eastern and central [[South America]]. Typically it is known simply as the '''crowned eagle''', which leads to potential confusion with the African ''[[Stephanoaetus coronatus]]''.  Due to its rarity, not much is known about its biology or population.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal |last1=Canal |first1=David |last2=Roques |first2=Séverine |last3=Negro |first3=Juan J. |last4=Sarasola |first4=José H. |title=Population genetics of the endangered Crowned Solitary Eagle (Buteogallus coronatus) in South America |journal=Conservation Genetics |date=February 2017 |volume=18 |issue=1 |pages=235–240 |doi=10.1007/s10592-016-0878-6 |bibcode=2017ConG...18..235C |hdl=10261/156337 |s2cid=21433835 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

== Description ==
The Chaco eagle is a large raptor with a mostly dull and silvery grey body plumage. Its short black tail has a broad white medial band as well as a white tip and both its [[cere]] and legs are yellow.<ref name=":0">{{cite book |last1=Ferguson-Lees |first1=James |last2=Christie |first2=David A. |title=Raptors of the World |date=2001 |publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |isbn=978-0-618-12762-7 }}{{page needed|date=November 2022}}</ref> As its name indicates, the Crowned Solitary Eagle has a crest of darker grey feathers.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal |last1=Do |first1=Eric |editor-first1=Thomas S |editor-last1=Schulenberg |title=Chaco Eagle (Buteogallus coronatus) |journal=Birds of the World |date=4 March 2020 |doi=10.2173/bow.croeag1.01 }}</ref> The juvenile also has a crest but its colour dark brown, except for it creamy head and underbody which are both have grey streaks.<ref name=":0" /> It is one of the largest raptors in the [[Neotropical realm|Neotropics]] and normally weighs around {{convert|2.95|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. Its total length can reach {{convert|73|-|79|cm|in|abbr=on}} and it has a wingspan of {{convert|170|-|183|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":2" />

== Taxonomy ==
[[File:Buteogallus coronatus, Las Colonias, Santa Fe, Argentina 1.jpg|thumb|left|A Chaco eagle in flight, showing the distinctive tail pattern; Santa Fe, Argentina.]]
''Buteogallus coronatus'' is monotypic, with no subspecies. Vieillot first named it ''Harpyia coronata'' in 1817. It was subsequently reclassified by [[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]] in 1842 in the genus ''Harpyphaliatus'', as ''Harpyhaliatus coronatus''. At the time, the [[Solitary eagle]] (now ''Buteogallus solitarius'') was considered to be a subspecies of ''Harpyhaliatus coronatus'', until [[Phylogenetics|phylogenetic analyses]] of their DNA showed that they are, in fact, [[sister species]]. These analyses also indicated that another species, the [[Great black hawk]] (''Buteogallus anthracinus''), is also a sister species. They were all reclassified in the genus ''[[Buteogallus]]''.<ref name=":2" />

== Habitat and distribution ==

=== Distribution ===
This bird is [[Endemism|endemic]] to eastern and central South America, where it is present in southern and central [[Brazil]], [[Bolivia]], [[Paraguay]] and in [[Argentina]] south to northern [[Patagonia]].<ref name=":3">{{cite journal |last1=Sarasola |first1=José Hernán |title=Nesting of the Endangered Chaco Eagle (''Buteogallus coronatus'') on a Human-made Structure |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=March 2018 |volume=52 |issue=1 |pages=108–111 |doi=10.3356/JRR-17-20.1 |s2cid=90164367 }}</ref> The status of the Chaco Eagle is uncertain in [[Uruguay]], where it has not been seen for decades and is believed to be extinct.<ref name=":2" /> It has become [[Local extinction|regionally extinct]] in portions of its historical range, and is found in very low density throughout its range,<ref name=":4">{{cite journal |last1=Barbar |first1=Facundo |last2=Capdevielle |first2=Andrés |last3=Encabo |first3=Manuel |title=Direct Persecution of Crowned Eagles (''Buteogallus coronatus'') in Argentina: A New Call for Their Conservation |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=March 2016 |volume=50 |issue=1 |pages=115–120 |doi=10.3356/rapt-50-01-115-120.1 |hdl=11336/60073 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> making it one of the rarest and most threatened raptors in the Neotropical region.<ref name=":1" /> Only 250-999 mature individuals are left, while the total individual count of the species reaches 375-1499 birds.<ref name=":2" />

=== Habitat ===
The Chaco Eagle can be found in semi open habitats such as open mixed [[grassland]]s, brushlands, [[savanna]]hs, [[marsh]]es and open woodlands. Also, the presence of large trees in the habitat is very important for the Chaco eagle since they are used for nesting.<ref name=":2" />

=== Threats ===
The Chaco Eagle is considered endangered.<ref name=":4" /> Its population is declining, a trend that is most likely due to human activities.<ref name=":1" /> The Chaco Eagle populations are affected by many threats, including habitat degradation, collisions with human structures, and persecution. An example of the latter would be in Argentina, where Chaco Eagles are still killed due to the belief that they attack livestock.<ref name=":4" /> Between 1999 and 2014, 30 Chaco Eagles were brought to a rehabilitation center after they had been shot to protect livestock.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Madden |first1=Kristin K. |last2=Rozhon |first2=Genevieve C. |last3=Dwyer |first3=James F. |title=Conservation Letter: Raptor Persecution |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=9 May 2019 |volume=53 |issue=2 |page=230 |doi=10.3356/JRR-18-37 |s2cid=164599836 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2019JRapR..53..230M }}</ref> For such a small population, this can have a great impact on the species' chances at survival. This persecution attracted the attention of conservation groups, which lead to the creation of a national plan of plan of conservation for the Chaco Eagle in Argentina, the only country to do so so far.<ref name=":4" /> Another important factor is the electrocution of Chaco Eagles by power poles, which also has a sizeable impact on their populations. This is also a common problem that unfortunately affects many raptors.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sarasola |first1=José H. |last2=Galmes |first2=Maximiliano A. |last3=Watts |first3=Bryan D. |title=Electrocution on Power Lines is an Important Threat for the Endangered Chaco Eagle (Buteogallus coronatus) in Argentina |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=19 May 2020 |volume=54 |issue=2 |page=166 |doi=10.3356/0892-1016-54.2.166 |s2cid=218685776 }}</ref>

== Behavior ==
Often seen in solitary pairs or small groups of three,<ref name=":2" /> the Chaco Eagle is a tame [[Crepuscular animal|crepuscular]] species that forages during early hours.<ref name=":0" />

=== Vocalization ===
The Chaco Eagle can be recognized by its very powerful and shrill whistling call that can be heard 2 km away.<ref name=":0" />

=== Diet ===
Chaco Eagles still-hunt from perches.<ref name=":0" /> Its diet consists mostly of small to medium size mammals. It primarily preys on [[armadillo]]s such as [[pichi]]s (''Zaedyus pichiy'') and [[nine-banded armadillos]] (''Dasypus novemcinctus'') as well as [[Andes skunk]]s (''Conepatus chinga'').<ref name =Lobos>Lobos, Roberto Pereyra, et al. "Diet of the Crowned Eagle (Harpyhaliaetus coronatus) during the breeding season in the Monte desert, Mendoza, Argentina." Journal of Raptor Research 45.2 (2011): 180-183.</ref><ref name =Montalvo>Montalvo, Claudia I., et al. "Crowned solitary eagle (Buteogallus coronatus) as accumulator of armadillo osteoderms in the archaeological record? An actualistic taphonomic study for central Argentina." Quaternary International 391 (2016): 90-99.</ref> But other mammals such as [[rodent]]s, [[Opossum|possum]]s, [[European hare]]s (''Lepus europaeus''), and [[lesser grison]]s (''Galictis cuja'') are also taken.<ref name =Montalvo/><ref name = Capdevielle>Capdevielle, Andrés, et al. "DIFFERENT HUNTING METHODS OF HARPYHALIAETUS CORONATUS DURING REHABILITATION."</ref> Reptiles such as [[snake]]s, [[tortoise]]s, and [[lizard]]s also make up a smaller part of their diet. When hunting snakes, the eagles often remove the head first. Singular scales on the eagle's tarsus protected them from snake bites. Small lizards are caught directly with talons, but large lizards such as [[red tegu]]s (''Salvator rufescens'') and [[Argentine black and white tegu]]s (''Tupinambis merianae'') are captured by approaching them from the side, grabbing them by the hip with one foot and then by the head with the other, apparently to reduce the risk of being bitten or whipped by the tail.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=Giacomo>Giacomo, Alejandro G., Santiago F. Krapovickas, and Javier Pereira. Historia natural y paisaje de la Reserva el Bagual, provincia de Formosa, Argentina: inventario de la fauna de vertebrados y de la flora vascular de un área protegida del Chaco Húmedo. No. 4. Aves Argentinas/Asociación Ornitológica del Plata, 2005.</ref><ref name =Lobos/><ref name = Capdevielle/> Normally, this raptor kills or hunts its prey, but some individuals have been seen eating dead deer, sheep, armadillos, and roadkill.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=Giacomo/>

=== Reproduction ===
[[File:Naturalis Biodiversity Center - ZMA.AVES.3149 - Harpyhaliaetus coronatus Vieillot, 1817 - Accipitridae - skin specimen.jpeg|thumb|A mounted specimen of a juvenile Chaco eagle.]]
The Chaco Eagle is a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] species. Between August and October, a single white egg with grey spots is laid on a platform-like nest of sticks and leaves. The nest is constructed on the tallest trees of breeding territories, 10-13m above ground.<ref name=":2" /> Some eagles have been seen nesting on human made buildings in area where no trees were available.<ref name=":3" /> The egg hatches around 45 days after it was laid (November–December).<ref name=":2" /> The female stays near its offspring from the moment the egg is laid until the chick is old enough to be left alone for short periods of time. The male continuously brings food to the nest, while the female joins the hunting when the chick hatches and grows a bit.<ref name=":5">{{cite journal |last1=Galmes |first1=Maximiliano A. |last2=Sarasola |first2=José H. |last3=Grande |first3=Juan Manuel |last4=Vargas |first4=F. Hernán |title=Parental Care of the Endangered Chaco Eagle (''Buteogallus coronatus'') in Central Argentina |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=September 2018 |volume=52 |issue=3 |pages=316–325 |doi=10.3356/JRR-16-82.1 |s2cid=91853862 |doi-access=free |hdl=11336/81480 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> The offspring will stay in the nest for 65–70 days.<ref name=":2" /> Not much is known concerning the age of breeding, but it is estimated that they do not breed before they reach 3–5 years of age.<ref name=":5" />
